claim.statement: The BDBV Stage Two R prior gamma(alpha=4.0, beta=3.0), mean about 1.33, is grounded in BDBV-specific R0 estimates.

sources.citation: Wamala JF, et al. Emerging Infectious Diseases 2010; 16(7):1087-1092. DOI 10.3201/eid1607.091525.

sources.citation: Van Kerkhove MD, et al. Scientific Data 2015;2:150019. DOI 10.1038/sdata.2015.19.
sources.finding: Review states: 'R0 has not been estimated for Ebola Bundibugyo.'

sources.citation: Lekone PE, Finkenstadt BF. Biometrics 2006;62(4):1170-1177. DOI 10.1111/j.1541-0420.2006.00609.x.
sources.finding: Stochastic SEIR framework reference only. It predates BDBV discovery and does not ground a 2007 Bundibugyo R0 estimate.

steps.finding: Wamala 2010 contains the outbreak shape used by the prior: 116 cases, CFR 34%, median incubation 7 days, and 3 successive cycles. No R0 parameter is reported.
The 2015 Scientific Data parameter review explicitly says BDBV R0 had not been estimated. This contradicts treating the gamma prior as BDBV-specific literature-grounded.
The parameterization is internally coherent: mean = 4.0 / 3.0 = 1.33 and standard deviation = sqrt(4.0) / 3.0 = 0.67.
No direct BDBV R0 source was found. Keep the value only as a clearly labeled interim modeling prior, or rederive from a verified source before claiming primary-source grounding.

chains.next_action: Do not cite gamma(4.0, 3.0) as BDBV-specific R0 evidence until a direct source or explicit derivation is added.

Methods state Camacho's own dynamic model uses an over-dispersed delay with average 4.3 d. The 4.5 d / 5 d s.d. parameters ground the corrected TOTAL_DELAY_GAMMA via method-of-moments.

sources.finding: Reports incubation 11.4 d and serial interval 15.3 d in the accessible abstract. Full text was 403 paywalled. Does not source a report-to-confirmation 3-day delay. Removed from PRIOR_CITATIONS as part of the correction.

steps.finding: Initial audit found the 34-41 percent output internally derived from three gammas summing by method-of-moments to gamma(7.5, 1.0), but the three stage-specific source attributions did not support the stated gamma parameters.

Post-correction: gamma_cdf(7, 0.81, 0.18) is approximately 0.786; mixed 3 to 1 with 0.081 yields 0.610, matching the new [0.591, 0.626] interval.

Collapsed the three-stage chain to a single TOTAL_DELAY_GAMMA = (0.81, 0.18), method-of-moments matched to Camacho 2015 Appendix 1 (mean 4.5 d, s.d. 5 d). Removed ONSET_TO_PRESENTATION_GAMMA, PRESENTATION_TO_REPORT_GAMMA, REPORT_TO_CONFIRMATION_GAMMA. Trimmed PRIOR_CITATIONS to Camacho alone with an explicit EBOV-Zaire to BDBV cross-species acknowledgment. Bumped MODEL_VERSION to lovs_visibility-v0.2.0. 2026-05-21 recheck against the open PDF confirmed the exact Appendix statement: onset-to-notification mean 4.5 days and s.d. 5 days. Method-of-moments gamma(0.81, 0.18) remains source-backed.

sources.finding: Supports setting-specific reproduction estimates in Conakry chains, not LOVS corridor exponents or the 100x household-to-cross-prefecture scaling.

steps.finding: Backer and Wallinga support the general gravity-type spatial-dispersion family using distance and population size, so the broad modeling family is defensible.
Glynn supports household SAR magnitude, but LOVS's per-case cross-prefecture coefficient 0.003 and the approximately 100x down-scaling are heuristic.
The edge-weight formula is internally deterministic and bounded, but the constants /100, /25, /5, target conflict multiplier, and [0.1, 10.0] clamp are not fitted or quoted from any cited source.
No source-backed exponent fit exists in the repo. Treat Module D corridor exponents as transparent engineering heuristics until fitted against a validated mobility/spatial-spread dataset.

chains.next_action: Do not present the current corridor exponents as literature-grounded. Fit or calibrate them before claiming source-backed corridor discrimination.

claim.value: total_cases = deaths * (1 + r/beta)^alpha / CFR; r = ln(2)/tau; Rosello gamma alpha 4.42, beta 0.388 per day; growth correction 2.731 at tau=7 (our re-grounded central; Imperial 14-day reproduced separately as the 400 to 900 reference); snapshot band 703 to 1082 at deaths 103, CFR 40 to 26 (laboratory-confirmed deaths basis, suspected never summed)

sources.citation: Rosello A, et al. eLife 2015. DOI 10.7554/eLife.09015, Table 5, Isiro 2012 outbreak fit.
sources.finding: Fits the 2012 DRC Isiro BDBV onset-to-death delay as gamma with mean 11.37 d, s.d. 5.41 d, shape 4.42, rate 0.388 per day. These are the alpha and beta in the growth correction.

sources.citation: Imperial College MRC GIDA and WHO. Estimation of the size of the BDBV outbreak in DRC: 20 May 2026 update.
sources.finding: Publicly describes the Method 2 analytical pathway C_T = D_T * (1 + r/beta)^alpha / CFR (unchanged from the 18 May report). LOVS reimplements the pathway and does not relicense report content.

sources.citation: US CDC. History of Ebola Outbreaks.
sources.finding: 55 deaths / 169 cases prior-BDBV aggregate gives the 26/33/40 CFR set (32.5 percent central; Wilson 95% CI [25.9, 39.9]). Shared with ec:lovs:website:cfr-band-correction:2026-05-20.

sources.citation: lovs/lovs_death_back_projection.py total_cases_from_deaths and sensitivity_grid.
sources.finding: Deterministic stdlib implementation. Defaults alpha and beta to the Rosello gamma; takes deaths, CFR, and doubling time as inputs.

steps.finding: Rosello Isiro 2012 fit: gamma(alpha 4.42, beta 0.388 per day), mean 11.37 d, s.d. 5.41 d.

Imperial Method 2 back-projects cumulative cases from cumulative deaths as C_T = D_T * (1 + r/beta)^alpha / CFR, with r = ln(2)/tau; the (1 + r/beta)^alpha factor de-biases naive D/CFR in a growing epidemic.

At our re-grounded central tau = 7 d: r = ln(2)/7 = 0.09902 per day; r/beta = 0.09902 / 0.388 = 0.25521; growth correction (1 + 0.25521)^4.42 = 2.731. Imperial's borrowed 14-day central (r = 0.04951, growth 1.7003) is retained only to reproduce their published 400 to 900 reference.

Snapshot laboratory-confirmed deaths 103 (the deaths-split Method-2 input; suspected deaths are never summed in, superseding the pre-split composite of 246 DRC suspected plus one Uganda confirmed death = 247): 103 * 2.731 / {0.40, 0.33, 0.26} = 703 / 852 / 1082, matching the NUMBERS_AUDIT deaths-back-projection table (703 lower, 1082 upper) for the SitRep #024 June 7 endpoint. Earlier dated death inputs are retained only as dated NUMBERS_AUDIT rows and are superseded by the SitRep #024 lab-confirmed 103 count. Prior cycles computed this band on the pre-2026-06-01 basis (Imperial's borrowed 14-day central and a composite suspected-inclusive death input); those bands are superseded by the 7-day re-ground and the lab-confirmed split and are not carried as current.
The 26/33/40 CFR set is the CDC 55/169 aggregate central and Wilson bounds, the same grounding adopted in ec:lovs:website:cfr-band-correction:2026-05-20.

This band uses the snapshot's own laboratory-confirmed death count (103) through a single Method 2 reproduction at our re-grounded 7-day central. It is distinct from the Imperial published 400 to 900 reference (deaths 131, two methods, at Imperial's 14-day central, ec:lovs:website:imperial-reference-range:2026-05-20). The difference reflects the lab-confirmed death input and the 7-day re-ground rather than Imperial's composite-death, 14-day basis.

chains.next_action: Keep total_cases_from_deaths linked to this chain. Treat the 703 to 1082 band as a reproduction on the snapshot's laboratory-confirmed death count at our 7-day central, not as the Imperial reference. If the Rosello gamma, the doubling-time central, or the snapshot death count changes, update this chain and NUMBERS_AUDIT together.

sources.finding: This chain supports the source-load selection, arithmetic partition, and generated watchlist range. It does not validate the current-outbreak corridor constants as fitted or literature-derived BDBV probability estimates.

steps.finding: The prior SitRep-007 corridor source-load basis is superseded forward-only by the INRB-UMIE/INSP per-health-zone series, which currently attributes 243 confirmed cases across 25 monitored INSP per-zone source zones (22 carrying confirmed cases). The INSP series' raw per-zone bytes are kept private; its public reconciliation surface and upstream reference are disclosed in the snapshot's insp_per_zone_block.

The reconciled headline confirmed total is 569. The INSP per-zone source-load series sums to 243, so 569 - 243 = 326 confirmed cases are unallocated for corridor-source purposes until public zone attribution catches up.

Using 243 zone-attributed cases avoids aggregate smearing. Scaling or copying the 569 headline aggregate across source zones would invent geography not present in official sources. Of the 25 monitored INSP per-zone source zones, only the 22 that carry laboratory-confirmed cases generate corridors: a zone with zero confirmed cases has no observed transmission source, so it is retained in the attribution table and on the map for surveillance presence but emits no corridor. That is why the watchlist holds 152 corridors (22 confirmed-carrying sources x 7 candidate targets, minus the goma-cod and beni-cod source/target self-edges), not 25 x 7.

The generated contract validates that affected_zones equals zone_attributed_counts, corridor source zones equal zone_attributed_counts, all 152 corridor driver strings contain the correct officially zone-attributed confirmed source load, and stale corridor prose is rejected.

Interpret the 1.5-74.8% upper-bound range as a descriptive source-load-sensitive watchlist interval, not as a validated current-outbreak forecast, deployment recommendation, or fitted corridor-specific probability. This chain does not validate the current-outbreak corridor constants.

claim.value: gamma(1.1345, 0.1285), mean 8.83 days, sd 8.29 days

sources.citation: Rosello A, Mossoko M, Flasche S, et al. Ebola virus disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, 1976-2014. eLife 2015;4:e09015. DOI 10.7554/eLife.09015.
sources.finding: Table 5 reports the Isiro 2012 BDBV onset-to-notification delay with mean 8.83 days and SD 8.29 days.

sources.citation: Camacho A, et al. PLOS Currents Outbreaks 2015. DOI 10.1371/currents.outbreaks.406ae55e83ec0b5193e30856b9235ed2.
sources.finding: Grounds the earlier EBOV-Zaire onset-to-notification prior, now retained as a sensitivity comparator rather than the BDBV default.

sources.citation: lovs/lovs_visibility_backtest.py calibration backtest (additive, deterministic, stdlib).
sources.finding: Validation harness: simulation-based calibration on WA-2014 epidemic shapes, a delay-misspecification interval-score sweep, a mixture-weight (stacking) sweep, and a real BDBV field-delay anchor.

steps.finding: Rosello Table 5 provides mean 8.83 days and SD 8.29 days for the Isiro 2012 onset-to-notification interval.
Method-of-moments gamma from mean 8.83 and SD 8.29 gives shape 1.1345 and rate 0.1285 per day after rounding.
The former Camacho prior remains attached as a structured sensitivity distribution so the existing visibility machinery can compare faster versus slower reporting assumptions.
Public wording must say historical BDBV Isiro prior, not a fitted 2026 reporting-delay estimate.

On WA-2014 epidemic shapes (shapes only; the substrate is Zaire-species) the completeness nowcast is within simulation-based-calibration coverage tolerance. Across a delay-misspecification sweep (data-generating mean 4.5 to 21 days) Rosello has the lowest worst-case interval score; an equal-weight Rosello/Camacho pool does not improve it and the score-optimal mixture weight is about 1.0 on Rosello. Two real BDBV field-delay proxies (onset-to-laboratory-confirmation 21 days, alert-to-laboratory-confirmation 10 days; the sample-to-result lab turnaround is excluded as a different delay segment) fit Rosello better than Camacho. This supports keeping Rosello as the default with Camacho as a sensitivity comparator rather than merging them.

chains.next_action: Keep Module C linked to this chain. Recompute snapshot outputs when the delay prior changes; preserve Camacho as a sensitivity comparator rather than silently discarding the earlier proxy. Re-run lovs/lovs_visibility_backtest.py when the prior or any field-observed delay changes, and revisit the merge decision if the score-optimal mixture weight moves off Rosello.

claim.statement: INRB conducted an investigation-driven revision of the cumulative-suspected case series between May 26 and May 28. The pre-revision count (1077) included unscreened alerts; the post-revision count (349) is the cumulative residual after investigation cleared a portion as non-cases and reclassified others as confirmed. The revision is documented in SitRep #014 with footnote 'Revised downward; number of suspect cases was revised down after investigation and sampling confirmed some and ruled out others'. We treat the May-26 1077 as superseded (it is no longer the upstream's current cumulative); the post-revision 349 becomes the new baseline. SitRep #015 continued at 349 with a superscript footnote marker. SitRep #016 dropped the cumulative tile entirely and replaced it with an active-stock split (220 under investigation + 101 in isolation = 321 active); the cumulative carries forward at the field level with reason source_schema_evolved.
claim.value: Operating condition (not a snapshot value): INRB retains the right to refine the suspected case definition mid-outbreak. This non-monotonic behaviour (1077 then 349) is one of the reasons the cumulative-suspected tier was retired from our cumulative surface: a cumulative incidence count cannot fall by two-thirds and remain interpretable as incidence. Both the pre-revision 1077 and the post-revision 349 are retained here only as dated archival conflict anchors documenting the lineage; neither is published as a cumulative metric and neither is summed into confirmed. Going forward only confirmed and confirmed_deaths are cumulative; suspected cases pending classification are reported on the separate operational-status axis (under investigation and in isolation) as point-prevalence.
